project: Fluorinated Benzene Additives in High-Concentration Electrolytes

user_requests: Organize as many high-concentration lithium salt electrolytes as possible where ionic conductivity increases after adding fluorobenzene.
Identify electrolyte systems used to test all four additives and compile into a table.
Focus on 3M LiFSI DME system and collect as much conductivity vs additive amount data as possible.
assistant_actions: Matched additive concentration with conductivity values.
Identified 3M LiFSI DME as a common benchmark system.

user_requests: Find papers with DFT-calculated HOMO/LUMO energies for four fluorinated benzenes.
Find at least one for hexafluorobenzene with similar computational settings.
Ensure numerical HOMO/LUMO values are explicitly reported.
assistant_actions: Identified mismatch issues in reported data.
Suggested alternative approaches (calculation instead of relying solely on literature).

user_requests: Collect molecular weight, viscosity, dipole moment, dielectric constant with sources.
Can HOMO/LUMO be calculated in VS Code?
Teach step-by-step how to run xtb.
What to do after extracting xtb folders (bin/include/lib/share)?

sections.user_issue: Cannot find .xyz file type when saving.
assistant_solution: Ensure file is not saved as .xyz.txt.
Enable file extensions in Windows Explorer.

sections.user_question: Can dipole moment and viscosity be calculated the same way?

assistant_response.viscosity: No, requires experimental data or molecular dynamics simulation.
assistant_response.dielectric_constant: No, macroscopic property, not obtainable from single-molecule xtb.

assistant_analysis: Dipole moment correlates with dielectric constant.
Electrolyte conductivity changes are dominated by solvation structure, not viscosity.
Fluorination effect depends on substitution symmetry, not just number of fluorine atoms.

assistant_recommendation: Use xtb for HOMO/LUMO and dipole moment.
Use literature for viscosity and dielectric constant.
Combine electronic structure + physical properties to explain electrolyte behavior.
